is the specific [mode](/wiki/Mode_(literature) "Mode (literature)") of [fiction](/wiki/Fiction "Fiction") [represented](/wiki/Mimesis "Mimesis") in [performance](/wiki/Performance "Performance"): a [play](/wiki/Play_(theatre) "Play (theatre)"), [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera"), [mime](/wiki/Mime "Mime"), [ballet](/wiki/Ballet "Ballet"), etc., performed in a [theatre](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre"), or on [radio](/wiki/Radio_drama "Radio drama") or [television](/wiki/Television "Television").[[1]](#cite_note-elam98-1) Considered as a genre of [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry") in general, the dramatic mode has been contrasted with the [epic](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry") and the [lyrical](/wiki/Lyric_poetry "Lyric poetry") modes ever since [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle")'s *[Poetics](/wiki/Poetics_(Aristotle) "Poetics (Aristotle)")* (c. 335 BC)—the earliest work of [dramatic theory](/wiki/Dramatic_theory "Dramatic theory").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Drama** is the specific [mode](/wiki/Mode_(literature) "Mode (literature)") of [fiction](/wiki/Fiction "Fiction") [represented](/wiki/Mimesis "Mimesis") in [performance](/wiki/Performance "Performance"): a [play](/wiki/Play_(theatre) "Play (theatre)"), [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera"), [mime](/wiki/Mime "Mime"), [ballet](/wiki/Ballet "Ballet"), etc., performed in a [theatre](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre"), or on [radio](/wiki/Radio_drama "Radio drama") or [television](/wiki/Television "Television").[[1]](#cite_note-elam98-1) Considered as a genre of [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry") in general, the dramatic mode has been contrasted with the [epic](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry") and the [lyrical](/wiki/Lyric_poetry "Lyric poetry") modes ever since [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle")'s *[Poetics](/wiki/Poetics_(Aristotle) "Poetics (Aristotle)")* (c. 335 BC)—the earliest work of [dramatic theory](/wiki/Dramatic_theory "Dramatic theory").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The term "drama" comes from a [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") word meaning "deed" or "[act](/wiki/Action_(philosophy) "Action (philosophy)")" (δρᾶμα, *drâma*), which is derived from "I do" (δράω, *dráō*). The two [masks](/wiki/Mask "Mask") associated with drama represent the traditional [generic](/wiki/Genre "Genre") division between [comedy](/wiki/Comedy_(drama) "Comedy (drama)") and [tragedy](/wiki/Tragedy "Tragedy").

In English (as was the analogous case in many other European languages), the word *[play](/wiki/Play_(theatre) "Play (theatre)")* or *game* (translating the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *pleġan* or [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *ludus*) was the standard term for dramas until [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s time—just as its creator was a *play-maker* rather than a *dramatist* and the building was a *play-house* rather than a *[theatre](/wiki/Theater_(building) "Theater (building)")*.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The use of "drama" in a more narrow sense to designate a specific *type* of [play](/wiki/Play_(theatre) "Play (theatre)") dates from the modern era. "Drama" in this sense refers to a play that is *neither* a comedy nor a tragedy—for example, [Zola's](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Zola "Émile Zola") *[Thérèse Raquin](/wiki/Th%C3%A9r%C3%A8se_Raquin "Thérèse Raquin")* (1873) or [Chekhov's](/wiki/Anton_Chekhov "Anton Chekhov") *[Ivanov](/wiki/Ivanov_(play) "Ivanov (play)")* (1887). It is this narrower sense that the [film](/wiki/Film "Film") and [television](/wiki/Television "Television") industries, along with [film studies](/wiki/Film_studies "Film studies"), adopted to describe "[drama](/wiki/Drama_(film_and_television) "Drama (film and television)")" as a [genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") within their respective media. The term "[radio drama](/wiki/Radio_drama "Radio drama")" has been used in both senses—originally transmitted in a live performance. It may also be used to refer to the more high-brow and serious end of the dramatic output of [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The enactment of drama in [theatre](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre"), performed by [actors](/wiki/Actor "Actor") on a [stage](/wiki/Stage_(theatre) "Stage (theatre)") before an [audience](/wiki/Audience "Audience"), presupposes [collaborative](/wiki/Collaboration "Collaboration") modes of production and a [collective](/wiki/Collective "Collective") form of reception. The [structure of dramatic texts](/wiki/Dramatic_structure "Dramatic structure"), unlike other forms of [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature"), is directly influenced by this collaborative production and collective reception.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Mime is a form of drama where the action of a story is told only through the movement of the body. Drama can be combined with [music](/wiki/Music "Music"): the dramatic text in [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera") is generally sung throughout; in some ballets dance "expresses or imitates emotion, character, and narrative action."[[6]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_Britannica-6) [Musicals](/wiki/Musical_theatre "Musical theatre") include both spoken [dialogue](/wiki/Dialogue "Dialogue") and [songs](/wiki/Song "Song"); and some forms of drama have [incidental music](/wiki/Incidental_music "Incidental music") or musical accompaniment underscoring the dialogue ([melodrama](/wiki/Melodrama "Melodrama") and Japanese [Nō](/wiki/Noh "Noh"), for example).[[7]](#cite_note-cgt-7) [Closet drama](/wiki/Closet_drama "Closet drama") is a form that is intended to be read, rather than performed.[[8]](#cite_note-closet-8) In [improvisation](/wiki/Improvisational_theatre "Improvisational theatre"), the drama does not pre-exist the moment of performance; performers devise a dramatic script spontaneously before an audience.[[9]](#cite_note-impro-9)

## History of Western drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History of Western drama")]

### Classical Greek drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classical Greek drama")]

Main article: [Theatre of ancient Greece](/wiki/Theatre_of_ancient_Greece "Theatre of ancient Greece")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Relief_with_Menander_and_New_Comedy_Masks_-_Princeton_Art_Museum.jpg/250px-Relief_with_Menander_and_New_Comedy_Masks_-_Princeton_Art_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Relief_with_Menander_and_New_Comedy_Masks_-_Princeton_Art_Museum.jpg)

*Relief of a seated poet ([Menander](/wiki/Menander "Menander")) with masks of New Comedy*, 1st century BC – early 1st century AD, [Princeton University Art Museum](/wiki/Princeton_University_Art_Museum "Princeton University Art Museum")

[Western](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") drama originates in [classical Greece](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece").[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [theatrical culture](/wiki/Theatre_of_ancient_Greece "Theatre of ancient Greece") of the [city-state](/wiki/Polis "Polis") of [Athens](/wiki/Classical_Athens "Classical Athens") produced three [genres](/wiki/Genre "Genre") of drama: [tragedy](/wiki/Tragedy "Tragedy"), [comedy](/wiki/Comedy_(drama) "Comedy (drama)"), and the [satyr play](/wiki/Satyr_play "Satyr play"). Their origins remain obscure, though by the 5th century BC, they were [institutionalised](/wiki/Institution "Institution") in [competitions](/wiki/Agon "Agon") held as part of [festivities](/wiki/Athenian_festivals "Athenian festivals") celebrating the god [Dionysus](/wiki/Dionysus "Dionysus").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Historians know the names of many ancient Greek dramatists, not least [Thespis](/wiki/Thespis "Thespis"), who is credited with the innovation of an actor ("*hypokrites*") who speaks (rather than sings) and impersonates a [character](/wiki/Character_(arts) "Character (arts)") (rather than speaking in his own person), while interacting with the [chorus](/wiki/Greek_chorus "Greek chorus") and its leader ("*[coryphaeus](/wiki/Coryphaeus "Coryphaeus")*"), who were a traditional part of the performance of non-dramatic poetry ([dithyrambic](/wiki/Dithyramb "Dithyramb"), [lyric](/wiki/Lyric_poetry "Lyric poetry") and [epic](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry")).[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Only a small fraction of the work of five dramatists, however, has survived to this day: we have a small number of complete texts by the tragedians [Aeschylus](/wiki/Aeschylus "Aeschylus"), [Sophocles](/wiki/Sophocles "Sophocles") and [Euripides](/wiki/Euripides "Euripides"), and the comic writers [Aristophanes](/wiki/Aristophanes "Aristophanes") and, from the late 4th century, [Menander](/wiki/Menander "Menander").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Aeschylus' historical tragedy *[The Persians](/wiki/The_Persians "The Persians")* is the oldest surviving drama, although when it won first prize at the [City Dionysia](/wiki/Dionysia "Dionysia") competition in 472 BC, he had been writing plays for more than 25 years.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The competition ("*[agon](/wiki/Agon "Agon")*") for tragedies may have begun as early as 534 BC; official records ("*didaskaliai*") begin from 501 BC when the [satyr play](/wiki/Satyr_play "Satyr play") was introduced.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Tragic dramatists were required to present a [tetralogy](/wiki/Tetralogy "Tetralogy") of plays (though the individual works were not necessarily connected by story or theme), which usually consisted of three tragedies and one satyr play (though exceptions were made, as with Euripides' *[Alcestis](/wiki/Alcestis_(play) "Alcestis (play)")* in 438 BC). [Comedy](/wiki/Comedy "Comedy") was officially recognized with a prize in the competition from 487 to 486 BC.

Five comic dramatists competed at the City [Dionysia](/wiki/Dionysia "Dionysia") (though during the [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War") this may have been reduced to three), each offering a single comedy.[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Ancient Greek comedy](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_comedy "Ancient Greek comedy") is traditionally divided between "old comedy" (5th century BC), "middle comedy" (4th century BC) and "new comedy" (late 4th century to 2nd BC).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Classical Roman drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Classical Roman drama")]

Main article: [Theatre of ancient Rome](/wiki/Theatre_of_ancient_Rome "Theatre of ancient Rome")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Statuette_actor_Petit_Palais_ADUT00192.jpg/250px-Statuette_actor_Petit_Palais_ADUT00192.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statuette_actor_Petit_Palais_ADUT00192.jpg)

An ivory statuette of a Roman actor of [tragedy](/wiki/Tragedy "Tragedy"), 1st century AD

Following the expansion of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") (527–509 BC) into several Greek territories between 270 and 240 BC, Rome encountered [Greek drama](/wiki/Theatre_of_ancient_Greece "Theatre of ancient Greece").[[18]](#cite_note-h43-18) From the later years of the republic and by means of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") (27 BC–476 AD), theatre spread west across Europe, around the Mediterranean and reached England; [Roman theatre](/wiki/Theater_of_ancient_Rome "Theater of ancient Rome") was more varied, extensive and sophisticated than that of any culture before it.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

While Greek drama continued to be performed throughout the Roman period, the year 240 BC marks the beginning of regular [Roman drama](/wiki/Theatre_of_ancient_Rome "Theatre of ancient Rome").[[20]](#cite_note-20) From the beginning of the empire, however, interest in full-length drama declined in favour of a broader variety of theatrical entertainments.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The first important works of [Roman literature](/wiki/Latin_literature "Latin literature") were the [tragedies](/wiki/Tragedy "Tragedy") and [comedies](/wiki/Comedy_(drama) "Comedy (drama)") that [Livius Andronicus](/wiki/Livius_Andronicus "Livius Andronicus") wrote from 240 BC.[[22]](#cite_note-h47-22) Five years later, [Gnaeus Naevius](/wiki/Gnaeus_Naevius "Gnaeus Naevius") also began to write drama.[[22]](#cite_note-h47-22) No plays from either writer have survived. While both dramatists composed in both [genres](/wiki/Genres "Genres"), Andronicus was most appreciated for his tragedies and Naevius for his comedies; their successors tended to specialise in one or the other, which led to a separation of the subsequent development of each type of drama.[[22]](#cite_note-h47-22)

By the beginning of the 2nd century BC, drama was firmly established in Rome and a [guild](/wiki/Guild "Guild") of writers (*collegium poetarum*) had been formed.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The Roman comedies that have survived are all *[fabula palliata](/wiki/Fabula_palliata "Fabula palliata")* (comedies based on Greek subjects) and come from two dramatists: [Titus Maccius Plautus](/wiki/Plautus "Plautus") (Plautus) and [Publius Terentius Afer](/wiki/Terence "Terence") (Terence).[[24]](#cite_note-24) In re-working the Greek originals, the Roman comic dramatists abolished the role of the [chorus](/wiki/Greek_chorus "Greek chorus") in dividing the drama into [episodes](/wiki/Episode "Episode") and introduced musical accompaniment to its [dialogue](/wiki/Dialogue "Dialogue") (between one-third of the dialogue in the comedies of Plautus and two-thirds in those of Terence).[[25]](#cite_note-h49-25) The action of all scenes is set in the exterior location of a street and its complications often follow from [eavesdropping](/wiki/Eavesdropping "Eavesdropping").[[25]](#cite_note-h49-25)

Plautus, the more popular of the two, wrote between 205 and 184 BC and twenty of his comedies survive, of which his [farces](/wiki/Farce "Farce") are best known; he was admired for the [wit](/wiki/Wit "Wit") of his dialogue and his use of a variety of [poetic meters](/wiki/Meter_(poetry) "Meter (poetry)").[[26]](#cite_note-h48-26) All of the six comedies that Terence wrote between 166 and 160 BC have survived; the complexity of his plots, in which he often combined several Greek originals, was sometimes denounced, but his double-plots enabled a sophisticated presentation of contrasting human behaviour.[[26]](#cite_note-h48-26) No early Roman tragedy survives, though it was highly regarded in its day; historians know of three early tragedians—[Quintus Ennius](/wiki/Quintus_Ennius "Quintus Ennius"), [Marcus Pacuvius](/wiki/Marcus_Pacuvius "Marcus Pacuvius"), and [Lucius Accius](/wiki/Lucius_Accius "Lucius Accius").[[25]](#cite_note-h49-25)

From the time of the empire, the work of two tragedians survives—one is an unknown author, while the other is the Stoic philosopher [Seneca](/wiki/Seneca_the_Younger "Seneca the Younger").[[27]](#cite_note-h50-27) Nine of Seneca's tragedies survive, all of which are *fabula crepidata* (tragedies adapted from Greek originals); his *[Phaedra](/wiki/Phaedra_(Seneca) "Phaedra (Seneca)")*, for example, was based on [Euripides](/wiki/Euripides "Euripides")' *[Hippolytus](/wiki/Hippolytus_(play) "Hippolytus (play)")*.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Historians do not know who wrote the only [extant](/wiki/Extant_literature "Extant literature") example of the *fabula praetexta* (tragedies based on Roman subjects), *[Octavia](/wiki/Octavia_(play) "Octavia (play)")*, but in former times it was mistakenly attributed to Seneca due to his appearance as a [character](/wiki/Character_(arts) "Character (arts)") in the tragedy.[[27]](#cite_note-h50-27)

### Medieval

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Medieval")]

Main article: [Medieval theatre](/wiki/Medieval_theatre "Medieval theatre")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Castle_of_Perseverance_staging.jpg/250px-Castle_of_Perseverance_staging.jpg)](/wiki/File:Castle_of_Perseverance_staging.jpg)

Stage drawing from a 15th-century vernacular [morality play](/wiki/Morality_play "Morality play") *[The Castle of Perseverance](/wiki/The_Castle_of_Perseverance "The Castle of Perseverance")* (as found in the [Macro Manuscript](/wiki/Macro_Manuscript "Macro Manuscript"))

Beginning in the [early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), churches staged dramatised versions of biblical events, known as [liturgical dramas](/wiki/Liturgical_drama "Liturgical drama"), to enliven annual celebrations.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The earliest example is the [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") trope *Whom do you Seek? (Quem-Quaeritis)* (c. 925).[[30]](#cite_note-Brockett_76-30) Two groups would sing responsively in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), though no impersonation of [characters](/wiki/Character_(arts) "Character (arts)") was involved. By the 11th century, it had spread through Europe to [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), and [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"); excluding [Islamic-era Spain](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus").

In the 10th century, [Hrosvitha](/wiki/Hrosvitha "Hrosvitha") wrote six plays in Latin modeled on [Terence](/wiki/Terence "Terence")'s comedies, but which treated religious subjects.[[31]](#cite_note-Brockett_Hildy,77-31) Her plays are the first known to be composed by a female dramatist and the first identifiable Western drama of the post-Classical era.[[31]](#cite_note-Brockett_Hildy,77-31) Later, [Hildegard of Bingen](/wiki/Hildegard_of_Bingen "Hildegard of Bingen") wrote a [musical](/wiki/Music "Music") drama, *Ordo Virtutum* (c. 1155).[[31]](#cite_note-Brockett_Hildy,77-31)

One of the most famous of the early [secular](/wiki/Secularity "Secularity") plays is the courtly [pastoral](/wiki/Pastoral "Pastoral") *[Robin and Marion](/wiki/Jeu_de_Robin_et_Marion "Jeu de Robin et Marion")*, written in the 13th century in French by [Adam de la Halle](/wiki/Adam_de_la_Halle "Adam de la Halle").[[32]](#cite_note-32) *[The Interlude of the Student and the Girl](/wiki/The_Interlude_of_the_Student_and_the_Girl "The Interlude of the Student and the Girl")* (c. 1300), one of the earliest known in English, seems to be the closest in tone and form to the contemporaneous French [farces](/wiki/Farce "Farce"), such as *[The Boy and the Blind Man](/wiki/The_Boy_and_the_Blind_Man "The Boy and the Blind Man").*[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Many plays survive from [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") in the [late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages"), when some type of religious drama was performed in nearly every European country. Many of these plays contained [comedy](/wiki/Comedy_(drama) "Comedy (drama)"), [devils](/wiki/Devil "Devil"), [villains](/wiki/Villain "Villain"), and [clowns](/wiki/Clown "Clown").[[34]](#cite_note-34) In England, trade guilds began to perform [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") "[mystery plays](/wiki/Mystery_plays "Mystery plays")", which were composed of long cycles of many playlets or "pageants", of which four are [extant](/wiki/Extant_literature "Extant literature"): [York](/wiki/York_Mystery_Plays "York Mystery Plays") (48 plays), [Chester](/wiki/Chester_Mystery_Plays "Chester Mystery Plays") (24), [Wakefield](/wiki/Wakefield_Mystery_Plays "Wakefield Mystery Plays") (32) and the so-called "[N-Town](/wiki/N-Town_Plays "N-Town Plays")" (42). *[The Second Shepherds' Play](/wiki/The_Second_Shepherds%27_Play "The Second Shepherds' Play")* from the Wakefield cycle is a farcical story of a stolen sheep that its [protagonist](/wiki/Protagonist "Protagonist"), Mak, tries to pass off as his new-born child asleep in a crib; it ends when the shepherds from whom he has stolen are summoned to the [Nativity of Jesus](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

[Morality plays](/wiki/Morality_plays "Morality plays") (a modern term) emerged as a distinct dramatic form around 1400 and flourished in the early [Elizabethan era](/wiki/Elizabethan_era "Elizabethan era") in England. Characters were often used to represent different ethical ideals. *[Everyman](/wiki/Everyman_(15th-century_play) "Everyman (15th-century play)")*, for example, includes such figures as Good Deeds, Knowledge and Strength, and this characterisation reinforces the conflict between good and evil for the audience. *[The Castle of Perseverance](/wiki/The_Castle_of_Perseverance "The Castle of Perseverance")* (c. 1400–1425) depicts an archetypal figure's progress from birth through to death. *[Horestes](/wiki/Horestes "Horestes")* (c. 1567), a late "hybrid morality" and one of the earliest examples of an English [revenge play](/wiki/Revenge_play "Revenge play"), brings together the classical story of [Orestes](/wiki/Orestes "Orestes") with a [Vice](/wiki/Vice_(character) "Vice (character)") from the medieval [allegorical](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") tradition, alternating comic, [slapstick](/wiki/Slapstick "Slapstick") scenes with serious, [tragic](/wiki/Tragedy "Tragedy") ones.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Also important in this period were the folk dramas of the [Mummers Play](/wiki/Mummers_Play "Mummers Play"), performed during the [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") season. Court [masques](/wiki/Masque "Masque") were particularly popular during the reign of [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Elizabethan and Jacobean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Elizabethan and Jacobean")]

Main article: [English Renaissance theatre](/wiki/English_Renaissance_theatre "English Renaissance theatre")

One of the great flowerings of drama in [England](/wiki/England "England") occurred in the 16th and 17th centuries. Many of these plays were written in verse, particularly [iambic pentameter](/wiki/Iambic_pentameter "Iambic pentameter"). In addition to Shakespeare, such authors as [Christopher Marlowe](/wiki/Christopher_Marlowe "Christopher Marlowe"), [Thomas Middleton](/wiki/Thomas_Middleton "Thomas Middleton"), and [Ben Jonson](/wiki/Ben_Jonson "Ben Jonson") were prominent playwrights during this period. As in the [medieval period](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), historical plays celebrated the lives of past kings, enhancing the image of the [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_dynasty "Tudor dynasty") monarchy. Authors of this period drew some of their storylines from [Greek mythology](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology") and [Roman mythology](/wiki/Roman_mythology "Roman mythology") or from the plays of eminent Roman playwrights such as [Plautus](/wiki/Plautus "Plautus") and [Terence](/wiki/Terence "Terence").

### English Restoration comedy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: English Restoration comedy")]

Main article: [Restoration comedy](/wiki/Restoration_comedy "Restoration comedy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Colley_Cibber_as_Lord_Foppington_in_The_Relapse_by_John_Vanbrugh1.jpg/250px-Colley_Cibber_as_Lord_Foppington_in_The_Relapse_by_John_Vanbrugh1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colley_Cibber_as_Lord_Foppington_in_The_Relapse_by_John_Vanbrugh1.jpg)

[Colley Cibber](/wiki/Colley_Cibber "Colley Cibber") as the extravagant and affected Lord Foppington, "brutal, evil, and smart", in [Vanbrugh's](/wiki/John_Vanbrugh "John Vanbrugh") *[The Relapse](/wiki/The_Relapse "The Relapse")* (1696)

[Restoration comedy](/wiki/Restoration_comedy "Restoration comedy") refers to English comedies written and performed in England during the [Restoration](/wiki/Restoration_(England) "Restoration (England)") period from 1660 to 1710. [Comedy of manners](/wiki/Comedy_of_manners "Comedy of manners") is used as a synonym of Restoration comedy.[[38]](#cite_note-38) After [public theatre had been banned](/wiki/London_theatre_closure_1642 "London theatre closure 1642") by the [Puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") regime, the re-opening of the theatres in 1660 with the Restoration of [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") signalled a renaissance of [English drama](/wiki/English_drama "English drama").[[39]](#cite_note-39) Restoration comedy is known for its [sexual](/wiki/Human_sexual_behavior "Human sexual behavior") explicitness, urbane, cosmopolitan [wit](/wiki/Wit "Wit"), up-to-the-minute topical writing, and crowded and bustling plots. Its dramatists stole freely from the contemporary French and Spanish stage, from English [Jacobean](/wiki/James_I_of_England "James I of England") and [Caroline](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") plays, and even from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_theatre "Greek theatre") and [Roman](/wiki/Theatre_of_ancient_Rome "Theatre of ancient Rome") classical comedies, combining the various plotlines in adventurous ways. Resulting differences of tone in a single play were appreciated rather than frowned on, as the audience prized "variety" within as well as between plays. Restoration comedy peaked twice. The genre came to spectacular maturity in the mid-1670s with an extravaganza of [aristocratic](/wiki/Aristocracy "Aristocracy") comedies. Twenty lean years followed this short golden age, although the achievement of the first professional female playwright, [Aphra Behn](/wiki/Aphra_Behn "Aphra Behn"), in the 1680s is an important exception. In the mid-1690s, a brief second Restoration comedy renaissance arose, aimed at a wider audience. The comedies of the golden 1670s and 1690s peak times are significantly different from each other.

The unsentimental or "hard" comedies of [John Dryden](/wiki/John_Dryden "John Dryden"), [William Wycherley](/wiki/William_Wycherley "William Wycherley"), and [George Etherege](/wiki/George_Etherege "George Etherege") reflected the atmosphere at Court and celebrated with frankness an aristocratic [macho](/wiki/Machismo "Machismo") lifestyle of unremitting sexual intrigue and conquest. The [Earl of Rochester](/wiki/John_Wilmot,_2nd_Earl_of_Rochester "John Wilmot, 2nd Earl of Rochester"), real-life Restoration rake, courtier and poet, is flatteringly portrayed in Etherege's *[The Man of Mode](/wiki/The_Man_of_Mode "The Man of Mode")* (1676) as a riotous, witty, intellectual, and sexually irresistible aristocrat, a template for posterity's idea of the glamorous [Restoration rake](/wiki/Rake_(character) "Rake (character)") (actually never a very common character in Restoration comedy). The single play that does most to support the charge of [obscenity](/wiki/Obscenity "Obscenity") levelled then and now at Restoration comedy is probably Wycherley's masterpiece *[The Country Wife](/wiki/The_Country_Wife "The Country Wife")* (1675), whose title contains a lewd [pun](/wiki/Pun "Pun") and whose notorious "china scene" is a series of sustained [double entendres](/wiki/Double_entendre "Double entendre").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

During the second wave of Restoration comedy in the 1690s, the "softer" comedies of [William Congreve](/wiki/William_Congreve_(playwright) "William Congreve (playwright)") and [John Vanbrugh](/wiki/John_Vanbrugh "John Vanbrugh") set out to appeal to more socially diverse audience with a strong middle-class element, as well as to female spectators. The comic focus shifts from young lovers outwitting the older generation to the vicissitudes of marital relations. In Congreve's *[Love for Love](/wiki/Love_for_Love "Love for Love")* (1695) and *[The Way of the World](/wiki/The_Way_of_the_World "The Way of the World")* (1700), the give-and-take set pieces of couples testing their attraction for one another have mutated into witty prenuptial debates on the eve of marriage, as in the latter's famous "Proviso" scene. Vanbrugh's *[The Provoked Wife](/wiki/The_Provoked_Wife "The Provoked Wife")* (1697) has a light touch and more humanly recognisable characters, while *[The Relapse](/wiki/The_Relapse "The Relapse")* (1696) has been admired for its throwaway wit and the characterisation of Lord Foppington, an extravagant and affected burlesque [fop](/wiki/Fop "Fop") with a dark side.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The tolerance for Restoration comedy even in its modified form was running out by the end of the 17th century, as public opinion turned to respectability and seriousness even faster than the playwrights did.[[42]](#cite_note-42) At the much-anticipated all-star première in 1700 of *The Way of the World*, Congreve's first comedy for five years, the audience showed only moderate enthusiasm for that subtle and almost melancholy work. The comedy of sex and wit was about to be replaced by [sentimental comedy](/wiki/Sentimental_comedy "Sentimental comedy") and the drama of exemplary morality.

### Modern and postmodern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern and postmodern")]

The pivotal and innovative contributions of the [19th-century](/wiki/Nineteenth-century_theatre "Nineteenth-century theatre") Norwegian dramatist [Henrik Ibsen](/wiki/Henrik_Ibsen "Henrik Ibsen") and the [20th-century](/wiki/Twentieth-century_theatre "Twentieth-century theatre") German theatre practitioner [Bertolt Brecht](/wiki/Bertolt_Brecht "Bertolt Brecht") dominate modern drama; each inspired a tradition of imitators, which include many of the greatest playwrights of the modern era.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The works of both playwrights are, in their different ways, both [modernist](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") and [realist](/wiki/Realism_(theatre) "Realism (theatre)"), incorporating formal [experimentation](/wiki/Experimental_theatre "Experimental theatre"), [meta-theatricality](/wiki/Meta-theatre "Meta-theatre"), and [social critique](/wiki/Social_criticism "Social criticism").[[44]](#cite_note-44) In terms of the traditional theoretical discourse of genre, Ibsen's work has been described as the culmination of "[liberal tragedy](/wiki/Tragedy#Modern_development "Tragedy")", while Brecht's has been aligned with an [historicised](/wiki/Historicization "Historicization") comedy.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Other important playwrights of the modern era include [Antonin Artaud](/wiki/Antonin_Artaud "Antonin Artaud"), [August Strindberg](/wiki/August_Strindberg "August Strindberg"), [Anton Chekhov](/wiki/Anton_Chekhov "Anton Chekhov"), [Frank Wedekind](/wiki/Frank_Wedekind "Frank Wedekind"), [Maurice Maeterlinck](/wiki/Maurice_Maeterlinck "Maurice Maeterlinck"), [Federico García Lorca](/wiki/Federico_Garc%C3%ADa_Lorca "Federico García Lorca"), [Eugene O'Neill](/wiki/Eugene_O%27Neill "Eugene O'Neill"), [Luigi Pirandello](/wiki/Luigi_Pirandello "Luigi Pirandello"), [George Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw"), [Ernst Toller](/wiki/Ernst_Toller "Ernst Toller"), [Vladimir Mayakovsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Mayakovsky "Vladimir Mayakovsky"), [Arthur Miller](/wiki/Arthur_Miller "Arthur Miller"), [Tennessee Williams](/wiki/Tennessee_Williams "Tennessee Williams"), [Jean Genet](/wiki/Jean_Genet "Jean Genet"), [Eugène Ionesco](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Ionesco "Eugène Ionesco"), [Samuel Beckett](/wiki/Samuel_Beckett "Samuel Beckett"), [Harold Pinter](/wiki/Harold_Pinter "Harold Pinter"), [Friedrich Dürrenmatt](/wiki/Friedrich_D%C3%BCrrenmatt "Friedrich Dürrenmatt"), [Dario Fo](/wiki/Dario_Fo "Dario Fo"), [Heiner Müller](/wiki/Heiner_M%C3%BCller "Heiner Müller"), and [Caryl Churchill](/wiki/Caryl_Churchill "Caryl Churchill").

## Opera

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Opera")]

[Western opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera") is a dramatic art form that arose during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance")[[46]](#cite_note-46) in an attempt to revive the [classical Greek drama](/wiki/Theatre_of_Ancient_Greece "Theatre of Ancient Greece") in which dialogue, dance, and song were combined. Being strongly intertwined with [western classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music"), the opera has undergone enormous changes in the past four centuries and it is an important form of theatre until this day. Noteworthy is the major influence of the German 19th-century composer [Richard Wagner](/wiki/Wagner "Wagner") on the opera tradition. In his view, there was no proper balance between music and theatre in the operas of his time, because the music seemed to be more important than the dramatic aspects in these works. To restore the connection with the classical drama, he entirely renewed the operatic form to emphasize the equal importance of music and drama in works that he called "[music dramas](/wiki/Music_drama "Music drama")".

[Chinese opera](/wiki/Chinese_opera "Chinese opera") has seen a more conservative development over a somewhat longer period of time.

## Pantomime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Pantomime")]

Main article: [Pantomime](/wiki/Pantomime "Pantomime")

[Pantomime](/wiki/Pantomime "Pantomime") (informally "panto"),[[47]](#cite_note-47) is a type of [musical comedy](/wiki/Musical_theatre "Musical theatre") stage production, designed for family entertainment. It was developed in England and is still performed throughout the United Kingdom, generally during the Christmas and New Year season and, to a lesser extent, in other English-speaking countries. Modern pantomime includes songs, gags, slapstick comedy and dancing, employs gender-crossing actors, and combines [topical humour](/wiki/Topical_humour "Topical humour") with a story loosely based on a well-known fairy tale, fable or [folk tale](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore").[[48]](#cite_note-Reid-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) It is a participatory form of theatre, in which the audience is expected to sing along with certain parts of the music and shout out phrases to the performers. Part of the appeal of [amateur dramatics](/wiki/Amateur_dramatics "Amateur dramatics") pantomime productions is seeing well-known local figures on stage.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

These stories follow in the tradition of [fables](/wiki/Fable "Fable") and [folk tales](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore"). Usually, there is a lesson learned, and with some help from the audience, the hero/heroine saves the day. This kind of play uses [stock characters](/wiki/Stock_character "Stock character") seen in masque and again [commedia dell'arte](/wiki/Commedia_dell%27arte "Commedia dell'arte"), these characters include the villain (doctore), the clown/servant (Arlechino/Harlequin/buttons), the lovers etc. These plays usually have an emphasis on [moral dilemmas](/wiki/Moral_dilemma "Moral dilemma"), and good always triumphs over evil, this kind of play is also very entertaining making it a very effective way of reaching many people.

Pantomime has a long theatrical history in Western culture dating back to classical theatre. It developed partly from the 16th century [commedia dell'arte](/wiki/Commedia_dell%27arte "Commedia dell'arte") tradition of Italy, as well as other European and British stage traditions, such as 17th-century [masques](/wiki/Masque "Masque") and [music hall](/wiki/Music_hall "Music hall").[[48]](#cite_note-Reid-48) An important part of the pantomime, until the late 19th century, was the [harlequinade](/wiki/Harlequinade "Harlequinade").[[51]](#cite_note-HistoryIBY-51) Outside Britain the word "pantomime" is usually used to mean **[miming](/wiki/Mime_artist "Mime artist")**, rather than the theatrical form discussed here.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

## Mime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Mime")]

[Mime](/wiki/Mime_artist "Mime artist") is a theatrical medium where the action of a story is told through the movement of the body, without the use of speech. Performance of mime occurred in [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), and the word is taken from a single masked dancer called *Pantomimus*, although their performances were not necessarily silent.[[53]](#cite_note-53) In [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") Europe, early forms of mime, such as [mummer plays](/wiki/Mummers_Play "Mummers Play") and later [dumbshows](/wiki/Dumbshow "Dumbshow"), evolved. In the early nineteenth century [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), [Jean-Gaspard Deburau](/wiki/Jean-Gaspard_Deburau "Jean-Gaspard Deburau") solidified the many attributes that we have come to know in modern times, including the silent figure in whiteface.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[Jacques Copeau](/wiki/Jacques_Copeau "Jacques Copeau"), strongly influenced by [Commedia dell'arte](/wiki/Commedia_dell%27arte "Commedia dell'arte") and Japanese [Noh](/wiki/Noh "Noh") theatre, used masks in the training of his actors. [Étienne Decroux](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Decroux "Étienne Decroux"), a pupil of his, was highly influenced by this and started exploring and developing the possibilities of mime and refined [corporeal mime](/wiki/Corporeal_mime "Corporeal mime") into a highly sculptural form, taking it outside of the realms of [naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(theatre) "Naturalism (theatre)"). [Jacques Lecoq](/wiki/Jacques_Lecoq "Jacques Lecoq") contributed significantly to the development of mime and [physical theatre](/wiki/Physical_theatre "Physical theatre") with his training methods.[[55]](#cite_note-Callery-55)

## Ballet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ballet")]

Main article: [Ballet](/wiki/Ballet "Ballet")

While some ballet emphasises "the lines and patterns of movement itself" dramatic dance "expresses or imitates emotion, character, and narrative action".[[6]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_Britannica-6) Such ballets are theatrical works that have characters and "tell a story",[[56]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-56) Dance movements in ballet "are often closely related to everyday forms of physical expression, [so that] there is an expressive quality inherent in nearly all dancing", and this is used to convey both action and emotions; mime is also used.[[56]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-56) Examples include [Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky](/wiki/Pyotr_Ilyich_Tchaikovsky "Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky")'s *[Swan Lake](/wiki/Swan_Lake "Swan Lake")*, which tells the story of Odette, a princess turned into a swan by an evil sorcerer's curse, [Sergei Prokofiev](/wiki/Sergei_Prokofiev "Sergei Prokofiev")'s ballet *[Romeo and Juliet](/wiki/Romeo_and_Juliet_(Prokofiev) "Romeo and Juliet (Prokofiev)")*, based on Shakespeare's famous play, and [Igor Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky")'s *[Petrushka](/wiki/Petrushka_(ballet) "Petrushka (ballet)")*, which tells the story of the loves and jealousies of three puppets.

## Creative drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Creative drama")]

Creative drama includes dramatic activities and games used primarily in educational settings with children. Its roots in the United States began in the early 1900s. [Winifred Ward](/wiki/Winifred_Ward "Winifred Ward") is considered to be the founder of creative drama in education, establishing the first academic use of drama in Evanston, Illinois.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

## Asian drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Asian drama")]

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: India")]

Main article: [Theatre in India](/wiki/Theatre_in_India "Theatre in India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Macbeth_by_Kalidasa_kalakendram.JPG/250px-Macbeth_by_Kalidasa_kalakendram.JPG)](/wiki/File:Macbeth_by_Kalidasa_kalakendram.JPG)

A scene from the drama *Macbeth* by [Kalidasa Kalakendram](/wiki/Kalidasa_Kalakendram "Kalidasa Kalakendram") in [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam") city, [India](/wiki/India "India")

The earliest form of [Indian](/wiki/India "India") drama was the [Sanskrit drama](/wiki/Sanskrit_drama "Sanskrit drama").[[58]](#cite_note-RSZ12-58) Between the 1st century AD and the 10th was a period of relative peace in the [history of India](/wiki/History_of_India "History of India") during which hundreds of plays were written.[[59]](#cite_note-59) With the [Islamic conquests](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquest in the Indian subcontinent") that began in the 10th and 11th centuries, theatre was discouraged or forbidden entirely.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Later, in an attempt to re-assert indigenous values and ideas, village theatre was encouraged across the subcontinent, developing in various regional languages from the 15th to the 19th centuries.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The [Bhakti movement](/wiki/Bhakti_movement "Bhakti movement") was influential in performances in several regions. Apart from regional languages, [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") saw the rise of [Vaishnavite](/wiki/Ekasarana_Dharma "Ekasarana Dharma") drama in an artificially mixed literary language called [Brajavali](/wiki/Brajavali_dialect "Brajavali dialect").[[62]](#cite_note-62) A distinct form of one-act plays called [Ankia Naat](/wiki/Ankia_Naat "Ankia Naat") developed in the works of [Sankardev](/wiki/Sankardev "Sankardev"),[[63]](#cite_note-63) a particular presentation of which is called [Bhaona](/wiki/Bhaona "Bhaona").[[64]](#cite_note-64) Modern Indian theatre developed during the [period of colonial rule](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") under the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), from the mid-19th century until the mid-20th.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

#### Sanskrit theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sanskrit theatre")]

Main article: [Sanskrit drama](/wiki/Sanskrit_drama "Sanskrit drama")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/%E0%B4%95%E0%B5%82%E0%B4%9F%E0%B4%BF%E0%B4%AF%E0%B4%BE%E0%B4%9F%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%9F%E0%B4%A4%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%A4%E0%B4%BF%E0%B4%B2%E0%B5%86%E0%B4%B8%E0%B5%81%E0%B4%97%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%B0%E0%B5%80%E0%B4%B5%E0%B5%BB.jpg/250px-%E0%B4%95%E0%B5%82%E0%B4%9F%E0%B4%BF%E0%B4%AF%E0%B4%BE%E0%B4%9F%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%9F%E0%B4%A4%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%A4%E0%B4%BF%E0%B4%B2%E0%B5%86%E0%B4%B8%E0%B5%81%E0%B4%97%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%B0%E0%B5%80%E0%B4%B5%E0%B5%BB.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E0%B4%95%E0%B5%82%E0%B4%9F%E0%B4%BF%E0%B4%AF%E0%B4%BE%E0%B4%9F%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%9F%E0%B4%A4%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%A4%E0%B4%BF%E0%B4%B2%E0%B5%86%E0%B4%B8%E0%B5%81%E0%B4%97%E0%B5%8D%E0%B4%B0%E0%B5%80%E0%B4%B5%E0%B5%BB.jpg)

Performer playing [Sugriva](/wiki/Sugriva "Sugriva") in the [Koodiyattam](/wiki/Koodiyattam "Koodiyattam") form of [Sanskrit theatre](/wiki/Sanskrit_drama "Sanskrit drama")

The earliest-surviving fragments of [Sanskrit drama](/wiki/Sanskrit_drama "Sanskrit drama") date from the 1st century AD.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The wealth of archeological evidence from earlier periods offers no indication of the existence of a tradition of theatre.[[67]](#cite_note-Richmond516-67) The ancient *[Vedas](/wiki/Vedas "Vedas")* ([hymns](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn") from between 1500 and 1000 BC that are among the earliest examples of [literature](/wiki/History_of_literature#India "History of literature") in the world) contain no hint of it (although a small number are composed in a form of [dialogue](/wiki/Dialogue "Dialogue")) and the [rituals](/wiki/Ritual "Ritual") of the [Vedic period](/wiki/Vedic_period "Vedic period") do not appear to have developed into theatre.[[67]](#cite_note-Richmond516-67) The *[Mahābhāṣya](/wiki/Mah%C4%81bh%C4%81%E1%B9%A3ya "Mahābhāṣya")* by [Patañjali](/wiki/Pata%C3%B1jali "Patañjali") contains the earliest reference to what may have been the seeds of Sanskrit drama.[[68]](#cite_note-Richmond517-68) This treatise on [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") from 140 BC provides a feasible date for the beginnings of [theatre in India](/wiki/Theatre_in_India "Theatre in India").[[68]](#cite_note-Richmond517-68)

The major source of evidence for Sanskrit theatre is *[A Treatise on Theatre](/wiki/Natyashastra "Natyashastra")* (*Nātyaśāstra*), a compendium whose date of composition is uncertain (estimates range from 200 BC to 200 AD) and whose authorship is attributed to [Bharata Muni](/wiki/Bharata_Muni "Bharata Muni"). The *Treatise* is the most complete work of dramaturgy in the ancient world. It addresses [acting](/wiki/Acting "Acting"), [dance](/wiki/Dance "Dance"), [music](/wiki/Music "Music"), [dramatic construction](/wiki/Dramaturgy "Dramaturgy"), architecture, [costuming](/wiki/Costume_design "Costume design"), [make-up](/wiki/Theatrical_makeup "Theatrical makeup"), [props](/wiki/Theatrical_properties "Theatrical properties"), the organisation of companies, the audience, competitions, and offers a [mythological](/wiki/Hindu_mythology "Hindu mythology") account of the origin of theatre.[[68]](#cite_note-Richmond517-68)

Its drama is regarded as the highest achievement of [Sanskrit literature](/wiki/Sanskrit_literature "Sanskrit literature").[[69]](#cite_note-Brandonxvii-69) It utilised [stock characters](/wiki/Stock_character "Stock character"), such as the hero (*nayaka*), heroine (*nayika*), or clown (*vidusaka*). Actors may have specialised in a particular type. It was patronized by the kings as well as village assemblies. Famous early playwrights include [Bhasa](/wiki/Bhasa "Bhasa"), [Kalidasa](/wiki/Kalidasa "Kalidasa") (famous for *[Urvashi, Won by Valour](/wiki/Vikram%C5%8Drva%C5%9B%C4%AByam "Vikramōrvaśīyam")*, *[Malavika and Agnimitra](/wiki/M%C4%81lavik%C4%81gnimitram "Mālavikāgnimitram")*, and *[The Recognition of Shakuntala](/wiki/Abhij%C3%B1%C4%81na%C5%9B%C4%81kuntalam "Abhijñānaśākuntalam")*), [Śudraka](/wiki/%C5%9Audraka "Śudraka") (famous for *[The Little Clay Cart](/wiki/M%E1%B9%9Bcchakatika "Mṛcchakatika")*), [Asvaghosa](/wiki/Asvaghosa "Asvaghosa"), [Daṇḍin](/wiki/Da%E1%B9%87%E1%B8%8Din "Daṇḍin"), and [Emperor Harsha](/wiki/Harsha "Harsha") (famous for *[Nagananda](/wiki/Nagananda "Nagananda")*, *[Ratnavali](/wiki/Ratnavali "Ratnavali"),* and *[Priyadarsika](/wiki/Priyadarsika "Priyadarsika")*). *[Śakuntalā](/wiki/Abhij%C3%B1%C4%81na%C5%9B%C4%81kuntalam "Abhijñānaśākuntalam")* (in English translation) influenced [Goethe's](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe") *[Faust](/wiki/Goethe%27s_Faust "Goethe's Faust")* (1808–1832).[[69]](#cite_note-Brandonxvii-69)

#### Mobile theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Mobile theatre")]

A distinct form of theatre has developed in India where the entire crew travels performing plays from place to place, with makeshift stages and equipment, particularly in the eastern parts of the country. [Jatra](/wiki/Jatra_(theatre) "Jatra (theatre)") ([Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") for "travel"), originating in the [Vaishnavite movement](/wiki/Gaudiya_Vaishnavism "Gaudiya Vaishnavism") of [Chaitanya Mahaprabhu](/wiki/Chaitanya_Mahaprabhu "Chaitanya Mahaprabhu") in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), is a tradition that follows this format.[[70]](#cite_note-jat-70) [Vaishnavite](/wiki/Ekasarana_Dharma "Ekasarana Dharma") plays in the neighbouring state of Assam, pioneered by [Srimanta Sankardeva](/wiki/Sankardev "Sankardev"), takes the forms of [Ankia Naat](/wiki/Ankia_Naat "Ankia Naat") and [Bhaona](/wiki/Bhaona "Bhaona"). These, along with Western influences, have inspired the development of modern mobile theatre, known in [Assamese](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language") as [Bhramyoman](/wiki/Mobile_theatre_in_Assam "Mobile theatre in Assam"), in Assam.[[71]](#cite_note-:0-71) Modern Bhramyoman stages everything from [Hindu mythology](/wiki/Hindu_mythology "Hindu mythology") to adaptations of [Western classics](/wiki/Western_drama "Western drama") and [Hollywood movies](/wiki/Hollywood_movies "Hollywood movies"),[[72]](#cite_note-72) and make use of modern techniques, such as live visual effects.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Assamese mobile theatre is estimated to be an industry worth a hundred million.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The self-contained nature of Bhramyoman, with all equipment and even the stage being carried by the troop itself, allows staging shows even in remote villages, giving wider reach.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Pioneers of this industry include [Achyut Lahkar](/wiki/Achyut_Lahkar "Achyut Lahkar") and [Brajanath Sarma](/wiki/Brajanath_Sarma "Brajanath Sarma").

#### Modern Indian drama

[Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore") was a pioneering modern playwright who wrote plays noted for their exploration and questioning of nationalism, identity, spiritualism and material greed.[[74]](#cite_note-Banham1051-74) His plays are written in [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") and include *Chitra* (*Chitrangada*, 1892), *The King of the Dark Chamber* (*Raja*, 1910), *[The Post Office](/wiki/The_Post_Office_(play) "The Post Office (play)")* (*Dakghar*, 1913), and *Red Oleander* (*Raktakarabi*, 1924).[[74]](#cite_note-Banham1051-74) [Girish Karnad](/wiki/Girish_Karnad "Girish Karnad") is a noted playwright, who has written a number of plays that use history and mythology, to critique and problematize ideas and ideals that are of contemporary relevance. Karnad's numerous plays such as *Tughlaq*, *Hayavadana*, *[Taledanda](/wiki/Taledanda "Taledanda")*, and *Naga-Mandala* are significant contributions to Indian drama. [Vijay Tendulkar](/wiki/Vijay_Tendulkar "Vijay Tendulkar") and [Mahesh Dattani](/wiki/Mahesh_Dattani "Mahesh Dattani") are amongst the major Indian playwrights of the 20th century. Mohan Rakesh in Hindi and Danish Iqbal in Urdu are considered architects of new age Drama. Mohan Rakesh's Aadhe Adhoore and Danish Iqbal's *Dara Shikoh* are considered modern classics.

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: China")]

Main article: [Theatre of China](/wiki/Theatre_of_China "Theatre of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Stamp_of_USSR_2262.jpg/250px-Stamp_of_USSR_2262.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_of_USSR_2262.jpg)

A 1958 U.S.S.R. [postage stamp](/wiki/Postage_stamp "Postage stamp") commemorating [Guan Hanqing](/wiki/Guan_Hanqing "Guan Hanqing"), one of the great Chinese dramatists, who is renowned for his "[zaju](/wiki/Zaju "Zaju")" plays

Chinese theatre has a long and complex history. Today it is often called [Chinese opera](/wiki/Chinese_opera "Chinese opera") although this normally refers specifically to the popular form known as [Beijing opera](/wiki/Beijing_opera "Beijing opera") and [Kunqu](/wiki/Kunqu "Kunqu"); there have been many other forms of theatre in China, such as [zaju](/wiki/Zaju "Zaju").

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Japan")]

Main article: [Theatre of Japan](/wiki/Theatre_of_Japan "Theatre of Japan")

Japanese [Nō drama](/wiki/Noh "Noh") is a serious dramatic form that combines drama, music, and dance into a complete aesthetic performance experience. It developed in the 14th and 15th centuries and has its own musical instruments and performance techniques, which were often handed down from father to son. The performers were generally male (for both male and female roles), although female amateurs also perform Nō dramas. Nō drama was supported by the government, and particularly the military, with many military commanders having their own troupes and sometimes performing themselves. It is still performed in Japan today.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

[Kyōgen](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dgen "Kyōgen") is the comic counterpart to Nō drama. It concentrates more on dialogue and less on music, although Nō instrumentalists sometimes appear also in Kyōgen. [Kabuki](/wiki/Kabuki "Kabuki") drama, developed from the 17th century, is another comic form, which includes dance.

Modern theatrical and musical drama has also developed in Japan in forms such as *[shingeki](/wiki/Shingeki "Shingeki")* and the [Takarazuka Revue](/wiki/Takarazuka_Revue "Takarazuka Revue").
